steps.title: Identify what the hypothesis predicts
steps.content: The hypothesis states that female preference for subspecies-specific feather sounds will drive "further genetic and anatomical divergence" between the subspecies. Direct support must show this divergence actually growing over time — not just explain the current difference, but demonstrate it widening.

steps.title: Eliminate each wrong choice and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'The feathers located on the wings of the migratory fork-tailed flycatchers have a narrower shape than those of the nonmigratory birds, which allows them to fly long distances.': About wing shape and flight distance, not tail feather sounds or female mate preference — does not address the hypothesis at all. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Over several generations, the sound made by the feathers of migratory male fork-tailed flycatchers grows progressively higher pitched relative to that made by the feathers of nonmigratory males.': Shows the acoustic difference between subspecies actively widening over generations — exactly the increasing divergence the hypothesis predicts. ✓
  • Choice C — 'Fork-tailed flycatchers communicate different messages to each other depending on whether their feathers create high-pitched or low-pitched sounds.': About communication functions of pitch, not about divergence between subspecies or female preference driving that divergence. ✗
  • Choice D — 'The breeding habits of the migratory and nonmigratory fork-tailed flycatchers remained generally the same over several generations.': Stable breeding habits suggest no divergence — the opposite of what the hypothesis predicts. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
